summary: This PR introduces a `should_strip_auth` method to `sessions.py` that strips the `Authorization` header whenever the root URL changes on redirect (different host, scheme, or port), with a specific carve-out for standard http→https upgrades. The logic appears correct for all tested cases, but several test-coverage gaps and minor style inconsistencies exist. No critical security vulnerabilities were identified. (1 finding(s) below the 0.4 confidence threshold hidden.)

findings.title: Port-only change (same scheme, different port) not covered by any test
findings.detail: The final `return` on line 132 fires when scheme is unchanged but port differs (e.g. `http://example.com:1234/` → `http://example.com:4321/`). The existing `test_should_strip_auth_port_change` actually exercises a case where BOTH scheme AND port differ (http→https with ports 1234→4321), meaning it hits the scheme-change part of the condition rather than the port-only-change path. A dedicated test such as `assert s.should_strip_auth('http://example.com:1234/foo', 'http://example.com:4321/bar')` is missing.

findings.title: Missing integration-level test for auth retention on same-host http→https redirect at standard ports
findings.detail: The http→https upgrade special-case branch (lines 128–130) is exercised only by unit-level `should_strip_auth` assertions. There is no integration-level test (e.g. using real or mocked HTTP fixtures) verifying that auth headers are actually retained end-to-end when a session follows a same-host http→https redirect at standard ports. Additionally, the test for explicit standard ports on both ends (`http://example.com:80` → `https://example.com:443`) is not present as a combined assertion.

findings.title: should_strip_auth does not handle missing hostname (relative/malformed URLs)
findings.detail: If `old_url` or `new_url` is a relative URL or otherwise lacks a scheme/host (e.g. a bare path like `/redirect`), `urlparse` will return `hostname=None` and `scheme=''`. The comparison `old_parsed.hostname != new_parsed.hostname` would then be `None != None` → `False`, skipping the hostname check and falling through to scheme/port comparisons. This could result in `should_strip_auth` returning `False` (keep auth) even when redirected to a different effective host, if the redirect URL is not fully resolved before `rebuild_auth` is called. It should be verified in `resolve_redirects` that all URLs are fully resolved/absolutized before `rebuild_auth` / `should_strip_auth` is invoked; if not, a guard or assertion should be added.

findings.title: No test for should_strip_auth with an https→https cross-host redirect
findings.detail: The hostname-change branch (lines 122–123) is only tested via `test_should_strip_auth_host_change`, which uses http→http URLs. There is no test covering an https→https cross-host redirect (e.g. `should_strip_auth('https://example.com/foo', 'https://other.com/bar')`) to confirm the branch returns `True` under HTTPS as well.

findings.title: No test for rebuild_auth when Authorization header is absent but should_strip_auth returns True
findings.detail: The `rebuild_auth` method gates the `should_strip_auth` call behind `'Authorization' in headers`. There is no test verifying that `rebuild_auth` behaves correctly (i.e., does not crash and does not inadvertently strip other state) when there is no Authorization header but `should_strip_auth` would return `True`. The combined integration of the guard condition and the new method is untested.

findings.title: Port normalization: same-endpoint URLs with explicit vs implicit default ports unnecessarily strip auth
findings.detail: When redirecting from `http://example.com/foo` to `http://example.com:80/foo` (or vice versa), both URLs point to the same effective endpoint, but the final `return` on line 132 evaluates `old_parsed.port (None) != new_parsed.port (80)` → `True`, causing auth to be stripped. This is an over-stripping (safe) rather than under-stripping case, so it's not a security vulnerability, but it may surprise users. Normalizing default ports (80 for http, 443 for https) before comparison would fix this. Note: the existing http→https special-case already handles this for the upgrade scenario.

findings.title: Docstring missing trailing period
findings.detail: The new docstring `"""Decide whether Authorization header should be removed when redirecting"""` is missing a trailing period, inconsistent with the convention used by other docstrings in this file (e.g. `"""Receives a Response. Returns a generator of Responses or Requests."""`).

findings.title: Logic review: no actual bugs found in scheme/port/hostname comparisons
findings.detail: Multiple reviewers examined the hostname comparison (case-sensitivity), the http→https special-case branch ordering, and the https→http downgrade path. All concluded the logic is correct: `urlparse().hostname` already lowercases hostnames and schemes per RFC 3986, the hostname check correctly fires before the scheme/port special-case, and no cross-host credential leakage is possible via the upgrade exception. No action required.

findings.title: Boolean operator placement inconsistent with surrounding style
findings.detail: The multi-line condition at lines 128–130 places `and` at the start of line 129, while the surrounding code in this file generally places boolean operators at the end of lines. Consider moving `and` to the end of each line for consistency with the rest of the file.

findings.title: test_should_strip_auth_https_upgrade groups multiple sub-cases without separation
findings.detail: In `test_should_strip_auth_https_upgrade` (lines 1603–1608), multiple `assert` statements for distinct sub-cases are grouped without blank lines or individual comments, making them harder to scan. The existing test style in this file typically isolates each logical scenario. Consider splitting non-standard-port cases into a dedicated test (e.g. `test_should_strip_auth_https_upgrade_nonstandard_port`) to align with the one-concept-per-test convention visible elsewhere in the file.

findings.title: http→https upgrade on non-standard source port incorrectly strips auth
findings.detail:
The special-case "allow http→https" branch (lines 128-130) only checks `old_parsed.port in (80, None)` and `new_parsed.port in (443, None)`. When the old URL uses an explicit non-standard port (e.g. `http://example.com:8080/`) and the new URL is `https://example.com/` (standard 443), the code reaches line 132 and returns `True` (strip auth) because `old_parsed.port (8080) != new_parsed.port (None)`. This is consistent with the test at line 1607 (`assert s.should_strip_auth('http://example.com:8080/foo', 'https://example.com/bar')`), which shows this is intentional. However, the symmetric case `http://example.com/foo` → `https://example.com:8443/bar` also strips auth (line 1608), which is also tested as intentional. So the logic matches the stated intent.

The actual bug is the *https→https port change* path: `old_parsed.scheme == 'https'` and `new_parsed.scheme == 'https'` with different ports. The special-case block only matches `old == http` and `new == https`, so it falls through to line 132 which correctly returns `True`. That is fine.

Let me reconsider: `http://example.com:80` → `https://example.com:443`. `old_parsed.port` is `80` (not `None`), and the condition checks `old_parsed.port in (80, None)` — this is `True`. `new_parsed.port` is `443` (not `None`), and `new_parsed.port in (443, None)` — this is `True`. So this returns `False` (don't strip). The test at line 1604 confirms this is correct.

Actually the logic appears correct for all the tested cases. No bug here.

findings.title: should_strip_auth does not handle missing hostname (relative/malformed URLs)
findings.detail: When `old_url` or `new_url` is a relative URL or otherwise lacks a scheme/host (e.g. a bare path like `/redirect`), `urlparse` will return `hostname=None` and `scheme=''`. The comparison `old_parsed.hostname != new_parsed.hostname` would then be `None != None` → `False`, skipping the hostname check entirely, and falling through to the scheme/port comparison. This could result in `should_strip_auth` returning `False` (keep auth) even when redirected to a different host, if the redirect URL is not fully resolved before `rebuild_auth` is called. Whether URLs are always fully resolved before `rebuild_auth` is called should be verified in `resolve_redirects`.

findings.title: Hostname comparison is case-sensitive via urlparse.hostname
findings.detail: `urlparse(...).hostname` returns the hostname lowercased on Python 3, so `old_parsed.hostname != new_parsed.hostname` is effectively case-insensitive. This is correct. No bug, just confirming the behavior is fine.

findings.title: Case-insensitive hostname comparison not enforced in should_strip_auth
findings.detail: urlparse().hostname already lowercases the hostname per RFC 3986, so the hostname comparison itself is fine. However, the scheme comparison on line 132 (`old_parsed.scheme != new_parsed.scheme`) also benefits from urlparse lowercasing scheme, so this is consistent. No real issue here — just worth confirming no custom URL forms bypass this.

findings.title: should_strip_auth does not strip auth on http→http port change to port 80
findings.detail:
Consider a redirect from `https://example.com/foo` to `http://example.com:80/bar`. Here:
- old scheme=https, new scheme=http (downgrade)
- old port=None (443), new port=80

The special-case check on lines 128-130 fires only when `old_parsed.scheme == 'http'`, so it correctly falls through to the final `return` for https→http.

However, consider `http://example.com/foo` → `http://example.com:80/foo`: old_port=None, new_port=80. The final condition evaluates `None != 80 or 'http' != 'http'` → `True`, so auth IS stripped even though both URLs are effectively the same endpoint. This is an over-stripping case, not an under-stripping case, so it's not a security vulnerability — but a symmetric normalisation issue. The more dangerous direction: `http://example.com:80/foo` → `http://example.com/foo` (None vs 80) also strips unnecessarily.

These are logic issues but not security issues per se (over-stripping is safe). No action required from a security standpoint.

findings.title: http→https upgrade backward-compat exception could allow credential forwarding to a redirected host on non-standard port
findings.detail: The special case on lines 128–130 permits auth headers to be forwarded when redirecting from `http://example.com:80/` to `https://example.com:443/`. This is intentional and documented. However, the hostname check on line 122 correctly returns True (strip) before this code is reached when the host changes, so there is no cross-host leakage via this path. The logic ordering is correct.

findings.title: Authorization header check is case-sensitive
findings.detail: The check `if 'Authorization' in headers` relies on the headers dict being case-insensitive (requests uses CaseInsensitiveDict). This appears to be the existing behavior and CaseInsensitiveDict is used throughout requests, so this is not a new issue introduced by this PR. However, it is worth confirming that `prepared_request.headers` is always a CaseInsensitiveDict — if any code path produces a plain dict here, the check could be bypassed, leaving Authorization in the headers when it should be stripped. This is an existing risk not introduced by this PR.

findings.title: Docstring missing period at end
findings.detail: The existing docstrings in this file end with a period (e.g. `"""Receives a Response. Returns a generator of Responses or Requests."""`). The new docstring `"""Decide whether Authorization header should be removed when redirecting"""` is missing a trailing period for consistency.

findings.title: Implicit line continuation style inconsistent with surrounding code
findings.detail: The existing code uses backslash continuation or wraps inside parentheses differently. The multi-line condition here uses a bare `(` opened on the `if` line, which is fine, but the closing `)` is on a separate line at line 130. Looking at the existing style in `merge_setting` (lines 62-65), multi-line boolean conditions are wrapped with the operator at the end of the line and closing paren on its own line — this new code puts `and` at the start of line 129, which differs from the `and` placement at end-of-line style used in the surrounding code. Consider moving `and` to end-of-lines for consistency.

findings.title: Unit tests for `should_strip_auth` don't follow the class-based pattern but are consistent — however they lack blank lines between assertions within a test
findings.detail: In `test_should_strip_auth_https_upgrade` (lines 1603–1608), multiple `assert` statements are grouped without any separating blank lines or comments, making the four sub-cases harder to scan. The existing test style (e.g. `test_auth_is_retained_for_redirect_on_host`) typically has one logical assertion group per test. The inline comment on line 1606 partially helps, but having a dedicated test for the non-standard-port cases (similar to how `test_should_strip_auth_host_change` is its own test) would align better with the one-concept-per-test convention visible in the file.

findings.title: http→https upgrade on non-standard source port (e.g. 8080→443) not tested for strip=False path
findings.detail: The special-case branch at lines 128-130 only skips stripping when BOTH the old URL uses a standard HTTP port (80 or None) AND the new URL uses a standard HTTPS port (443 or None). test_should_strip_auth_https_upgrade covers the True side of the non-standard port cases, but there is no test for upgrading from http://example.com:80 → https://example.com:443 (explicit standard ports on both ends) as a single combined assertion, and more importantly, there is no test exercising the branch where old port is standard but new port is non-standard (http://example.com → https://example.com:8443) returning True — which is tested — but the symmetric case http://example.com:8080 → https://example.com:443 is also only partially covered as a True case. Additionally, there is no integration-level test (using real httpbin fixtures) verifying that auth is retained on a same-host http→https redirect at standard ports; only the unit-level `should_strip_auth` check exists for that branch.

findings.title: Port-only change (same scheme, different port) not covered by any test
findings.detail: The final return on line 132 fires when the scheme is the same but the port differs (e.g. http://example.com:1234 → http://example.com:4321 with the same scheme). test_should_strip_auth_port_change only exercises a case where BOTH scheme AND port differ (http→https with ports 1234→4321), so it actually hits the scheme-change part of the condition, not the port-only-change part. There is no test for same-scheme-different-port, e.g. should_strip_auth('http://example.com:1234/foo', 'http://example.com:4321/bar').

findings.title: No test for should_strip_auth when Authorization header is absent (rebuild_auth path)
findings.detail: The rebuild_auth method (line 255) gates the should_strip_auth call behind 'Authorization' in headers. There is no test verifying that rebuild_auth behaves correctly (i.e., does not crash and does not strip) when there is no Authorization header but should_strip_auth would return True — the combined integration of the guard condition and the new method is untested.

findings.title: No test for should_strip_auth with an https → https host change
findings.detail: The hostname-change branch (lines 122-123) is only tested via test_should_strip_auth_host_change which uses http→http. There is no test covering an https→https cross-host redirect to confirm the branch still returns True under HTTPS.

findings.title: Port-only change (same scheme, different port) not covered by any test
findings.detail: The final `return` on line 132 fires when scheme is unchanged but port differs (e.g. `http://example.com:1234/` → `http://example.com:4321/`). The existing `test_should_strip_auth_port_change` actually exercises a case where BOTH scheme AND port differ (http→https with ports 1234→4321), meaning it hits the scheme-change part of the condition rather than the port-only-change path.
